title: Anal Fissure
oneLiner: A painful linear tear in the anal mucosa, common in young adults, causing severe sharp pain on defecation and bright red bleeding.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Perianal abscess / Fournier’s gangrene** – single finding: systemic sepsis (fever >38°C, HR >100, lactate >2) with perianal swelling, erythema, or crepitus; this is not a simple fissure.
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – single finding: rapidly spreading perineal pain, purple/black skin discolouration, crepitus, and high NEWS2; requires immediate surgical debridement.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe sharp or tearing pain during and immediately after defecation (“passing broken glass”)
  - Bright red blood on toilet paper or coating stool (small volume)
  - Constipation due to fear of pain (avoidance cycle)
  - May have a sensation of a “lump” (sentinel pile) at the anal verge
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspect gently with buttocks separated – classic longitudinal tear in posterior midline (90% of cases); anterior midline more common in women post-partum
  - Sentinel skin tag (oedematous papilla) at distal end
  - Internal sphincter spasm on digital rectal examination (DRE) – examine with lubricating jelly; if too painful, defer until after analgesia
  - No induration, fluctuance, or external swelling (if present, think abscess)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Haemorrhoids (internal/external)** – less sharp pain, more bleeding (dripping or splashing), no sentinel pile, visible perianal lumps
- **Perianal abscess** – constant, throbbing pain; swelling, erythema, fever; tender on palpation; DRE may feel fluctuation
- **Anal fistula** – history of abscess or perianal discharge; intermittent pain; palpable cord or opening externally
- **Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn’s disease)** – atypical fissures (lateral, multiple, painless, deep); associated diarrhoea, weight loss, mouth ulcers
- **Anal cancer** – persistent lump, bleeding, pain; risk factors (HPV, smoking); indurated ulcer with everted edges
- **Splitter:** Direction of fissure – midline posterior/anterior → simple fissure; lateral, multiple, or painless → suspect IBD or malignancy
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Inspection of perianal area (patient lying in left lateral position)
  - Gentle DRE with lubricating jelly (if tolerated) to assess sphincter tone, exclude abscess/mass
  - Anoscopy (if tolerated) to visualise fissure and rule out haemorrhoids
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (check for anaemia, WCC for infection)
  - CRP (elevated if abscess or IBD)
  - If IBD suspected: ESR, albumin, stool calprotectin (if available)
- **Imaging:**
  - Not routine for acute fissure
  - If suspect abscess: transperineal ultrasound or MRI pelvis (first-line in complex perianal sepsis)
  - If atypical features: flexible sigmoidoscopy (to exclude IBD or cancer)
- **Special tests:**
  - Stool microscopy, culture, and sensitivity (if diarrhoea present)
- **Never-miss test:** **DRE** – failing to perform one may miss a perianal abscess or malignancy
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Pain relief: topical lidocaine 2% gel PRN applied to anal margin
  - Stool softener: lactulose 15 mL twice daily or docusate sodium 100 mg twice daily
  - Warm sitz baths (sit in warm water 10–15 min after defecation)
  - Avoid straining, encourage high fibre intake (e.g., fybogel 1 sachet twice daily)
  - If severe spasm: consider topical nifedipine 0.2% ointment (apply to anal margin twice daily) – [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admission rarely needed unless severe pain, inability to pass urine, or complications
  - If admitted: full pain control, stool softeners, encourage mobilisation, high fibre diet
  - Consider topical calcium channel blockers or GTN 0.4% ointment (if nifedipine unavailable)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - High fibre diet + adequate fluid (6–8 glasses/day)
  - Bulk-forming laxative (ispaghula husk 1 sachet twice daily) + osmotic laxative (lactulose 15 mL twice daily as needed)
  - Topical nifedipine 0.2% or diltiazem 2% ointment twice daily for 6–8 weeks (first-line medical therapy)
  - If chronic (>6 weeks) or non-healing: refer to colorectal surgeon for Botox injection (20–25 units into internal sphincter) or lateral internal sphincterotomy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 8 hours if NBM or dehydrated
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if not low risk)
  - Diet: soft/low residue initially, then high fibre; NBM only if planned surgery
  - Observations: routine vital signs 4-hourly (NEWS2 chart)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Nifedipine 0.2% ointment: apply 0.5 cm strip to anal margin twice daily for 6–8 weeks (some areas use 0.3% or 0.4%; [check local policy])
  - Stool softener: lactulose oral solution 15 mL twice daily, titrate to produce soft stool
  - Bulk-forming laxative: ispaghula husk (Fybogel) 1 sachet twice daily with water
- **Common alternatives:**
  - GTN 0.4% ointment: apply 0.5 cm strip to anal margin three times daily for 8 weeks (off-label; causes headache in ~30%)
  - Diltiazem 2% ointment (compounded) twice daily for 8 weeks – less headache than GTN
  - Botulinum toxin A injection: 20–25 units into internal sphincter (outpatient procedure)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - GTN: contraindicated with phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) – risk of severe hypotension; also in narrow-angle glaucoma, hypotension, hypovolaemia
  - Nifedipine: caution in hypotension; can cause headache, dizziness, flushing
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment required for topical agents
  - Lactulose: caution in renal impairment (risk of electrolyte disturbance with prolonged use)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **GTN ointment:** prescribing wrong strength (0.4% vs 0.2%) or applying too much (>1 cm strip) → severe headache, hypotension, syncope; always document “apply a 0.5 cm strip” and check for PDE5 inhibitor use
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Severe pain → reflex urinary retention (difficulty voiding)
  - Constipation → faecal impaction → overflow incontinence
  - Secondary infection of fissure (rare, usually superficial)
- **Chronic:**
  - Persistent pain >6 weeks (defines chronic fissure)
  - Sentinel pile (oedematous skin tag at distal end)
  - Hypertrophied anal papilla (at proximal end)
  - Anal stenosis (rare, from fibrosis)
  - Recurrent fissure
sections.Prognosis:
- 50–90% of acute fissures heal with conservative management (high fibre, stool softeners, topical ointments) within 6–8 weeks.
- Chronic fissures often require surgical intervention (Botox or sphincterotomy) with >90% healing rate; recurrence rate ~10%.
- Low mortality, but significant impact on quality of life due to pain and avoidance.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- “Always perform a gentle PR (with jelly) to confirm the fissure and exclude an underlying abscess or mass – this is the most important single step.”
- “Prescribe a high-fibre supplement AND a stool softener (e.g., fybogel + lactulose) from day one to break the pain–constipation cycle.”
- “Teach the patient to use warm sitz baths after each bowel movement for 10–15 minutes to relax the sphincter and aid healing.”
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- “Never assume a lateral, multiple, or painless fissure is simple – always consider IBD (Crohn’s) or malignancy, and arrange colonoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy.”
- “Do not prescribe GTN ointment without checking if the patient is taking sildenafil or other PDE5 inhibitors – risk of life-threatening hypotension.”
- “Avoid referring for surgery in acute fissure – always try at least 6–8 weeks of medical therapy first unless there is a complication like abscess.”
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening pain, fever, perianal swelling or discharge (may indicate abscess)
  - Inability to pass urine (acute urinary retention) – may need catheterisation
  - Heavy rectal bleeding (more than a few drops on toilet paper) – unusual for fissure
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2–4 weeks if no improvement; if chronic (>6 weeks), refer to colorectal outpatient clinic
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - Signs of sepsis (temperature >38°C, HR >100, lactate >2) with perianal tenderness – suspect abscess or necrotising fasciitis
  - Inability to pass urine despite simple measures (analgesia, warm bath)
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Septic shock (BP <90 mmHg systolic, lactate >4, altered consciousness)
  - Crepitus extending to perineum or buttocks – Fournier’s gangrene
  - NEWS2 >5 or rising lactate despite fluid resuscitation

rawMarkdown:
## Anal Fissure
**One-liner:** A painful linear tear in the anal mucosa, common in young adults, causing severe sharp pain on defecation and bright red bleeding.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Perianal abscess / Fournier’s gangrene** – single finding: systemic sepsis (fever >38°C, HR >100, lactate >2) with perianal swelling, erythema, or crepitus; this is not a simple fissure.
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – single finding: rapidly spreading perineal pain, purple/black skin discolouration, crepitus, and high NEWS2; requires immediate surgical debridement.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe sharp or tearing pain during and immediately after defecation (“passing broken glass”)
  - Bright red blood on toilet paper or coating stool (small volume)
  - Constipation due to fear of pain (avoidance cycle)
  - May have a sensation of a “lump” (sentinel pile) at the anal verge
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspect gently with buttocks separated – classic longitudinal tear in posterior midline (90% of cases); anterior midline more common in women post-partum
  - Sentinel skin tag (oedematous papilla) at distal end
  - Internal sphincter spasm on digital rectal examination (DRE) – examine with lubricating jelly; if too painful, defer until after analgesia
  - No induration, fluctuance, or external swelling (if present, think abscess)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Haemorrhoids (internal/external)** – less sharp pain, more bleeding (dripping or splashing), no sentinel pile, visible perianal lumps
- **Perianal abscess** – constant, throbbing pain; swelling, erythema, fever; tender on palpation; DRE may feel fluctuation
- **Anal fistula** – history of abscess or perianal discharge; intermittent pain; palpable cord or opening externally
- **Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn’s disease)** – atypical fissures (lateral, multiple, painless, deep); associated diarrhoea, weight loss, mouth ulcers
- **Anal cancer** – persistent lump, bleeding, pain; risk factors (HPV, smoking); indurated ulcer with everted edges
- **Splitter:** Direction of fissure – midline posterior/anterior → simple fissure; lateral, multiple, or painless → suspect IBD or malignancy

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Inspection of perianal area (patient lying in left lateral position)
  - Gentle DRE with lubricating jelly (if tolerated) to assess sphincter tone, exclude abscess/mass
  - Anoscopy (if tolerated) to visualise fissure and rule out haemorrhoids
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (check for anaemia, WCC for infection)
  - CRP (elevated if abscess or IBD)
  - If IBD suspected: ESR, albumin, stool calprotectin (if available)
- **Imaging:**
  - Not routine for acute fissure
  - If suspect abscess: transperineal ultrasound or MRI pelvis (first-line in complex perianal sepsis)
  - If atypical features: flexible sigmoidoscopy (to exclude IBD or cancer)
- **Special tests:**
  - Stool microscopy, culture, and sensitivity (if diarrhoea present)
- **Never-miss test:** **DRE** – failing to perform one may miss a perianal abscess or malignancy

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Pain relief: topical lidocaine 2% gel PRN applied to anal margin
  - Stool softener: lactulose 15 mL twice daily or docusate sodium 100 mg twice daily
  - Warm sitz baths (sit in warm water 10–15 min after defecation)
  - Avoid straining, encourage high fibre intake (e.g., fybogel 1 sachet twice daily)
  - If severe spasm: consider topical nifedipine 0.2% ointment (apply to anal margin twice daily) – [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admission rarely needed unless severe pain, inability to pass urine, or complications
  - If admitted: full pain control, stool softeners, encourage mobilisation, high fibre diet
  - Consider topical calcium channel blockers or GTN 0.4% ointment (if nifedipine unavailable)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - High fibre diet + adequate fluid (6–8 glasses/day)
  - Bulk-forming laxative (ispaghula husk 1 sachet twice daily) + osmotic laxative (lactulose 15 mL twice daily as needed)
  - Topical nifedipine 0.2% or diltiazem 2% ointment twice daily for 6–8 weeks (first-line medical therapy)
  - If chronic (>6 weeks) or non-healing: refer to colorectal surgeon for Botox injection (20–25 units into internal sphincter) or lateral internal sphincterotomy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 8 hours if NBM or dehydrated
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if not low risk)
  - Diet: soft/low residue initially, then high fibre; NBM only if planned surgery
  - Observations: routine vital signs 4-hourly (NEWS2 chart)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Nifedipine 0.2% ointment: apply 0.5 cm strip to anal margin twice daily for 6–8 weeks (some areas use 0.3% or 0.4%; [check local policy])
  - Stool softener: lactulose oral solution 15 mL twice daily, titrate to produce soft stool
  - Bulk-forming laxative: ispaghula husk (Fybogel) 1 sachet twice daily with water
- **Common alternatives:**
  - GTN 0.4% ointment: apply 0.5 cm strip to anal margin three times daily for 8 weeks (off-label; causes headache in ~30%)
  - Diltiazem 2% ointment (compounded) twice daily for 8 weeks – less headache than GTN
  - Botulinum toxin A injection: 20–25 units into internal sphincter (outpatient procedure)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - GTN: contraindicated with phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) – risk of severe hypotension; also in narrow-angle glaucoma, hypotension, hypovolaemia
  - Nifedipine: caution in hypotension; can cause headache, dizziness, flushing
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment required for topical agents
  - Lactulose: caution in renal impairment (risk of electrolyte disturbance with prolonged use)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **GTN ointment:** prescribing wrong strength (0.4% vs 0.2%) or applying too much (>1 cm strip) → severe headache, hypotension, syncope; always document “apply a 0.5 cm strip” and check for PDE5 inhibitor use

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Severe pain → reflex urinary retention (difficulty voiding)
  - Constipation → faecal impaction → overflow incontinence
  - Secondary infection of fissure (rare, usually superficial)
- **Chronic:**
  - Persistent pain >6 weeks (defines chronic fissure)
  - Sentinel pile (oedematous skin tag at distal end)
  - Hypertrophied anal papilla (at proximal end)
  - Anal stenosis (rare, from fibrosis)
  - Recurrent fissure

### Prognosis
- 50–90% of acute fissures heal with conservative management (high fibre, stool softeners, topical ointments) within 6–8 weeks.
- Chronic fissures often require surgical intervention (Botox or sphincterotomy) with >90% healing rate; recurrence rate ~10%.
- Low mortality, but significant impact on quality of life due to pain and avoidance.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- “Always perform a gentle PR (with jelly) to confirm the fissure and exclude an underlying abscess or mass – this is the most important single step.”
- “Prescribe a high-fibre supplement AND a stool softener (e.g., fybogel + lactulose) from day one to break the pain–constipation cycle.”
- “Teach the patient to use warm sitz baths after each bowel movement for 10–15 minutes to relax the sphincter and aid healing.”

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- “Never assume a lateral, multiple, or painless fissure is simple – always consider IBD (Crohn’s) or malignancy, and arrange colonoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy.”
- “Do not prescribe GTN ointment without checking if the patient is taking sildenafil or other PDE5 inhibitors – risk of life-threatening hypotension.”
- “Avoid referring for surgery in acute fissure – always try at least 6–8 weeks of medical therapy first unless there is a complication like abscess.”

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening pain, fever, perianal swelling or discharge (may indicate abscess)
  - Inability to pass urine (acute urinary retention) – may need catheterisation
  - Heavy rectal bleeding (more than a few drops on toilet paper) – unusual for fissure
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2–4 weeks if no improvement; if chronic (>6 weeks), refer to colorectal outpatient clinic

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - Signs of sepsis (temperature >38°C, HR >100, lactate >2) with perianal tenderness – suspect abscess or necrotising fasciitis
  - Inability to pass urine despite simple measures (analgesia, warm bath)
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Septic shock (BP <90 mmHg systolic, lactate >4, altered consciousness)
  - Crepitus extending to perineum or buttocks – Fournier’s gangrene
  - NEWS2 >5 or rising lactate despite fluid resuscitation
